docs.title: Home
docs.text: ⚡ Query Compiler Engine

docs.title: LLM-powered queries.Deterministic SQL. Zero trust.
docs.text:
QCE compiles structured QueryPlan JSON into parameterized Postgres SQL — enforcing your schema allowlist, neutralizing injections, and guaranteeing identical output on every run. Let your LLM own intent. Let QCE own SQL.

→ Get Started How it works GitHub Python ≥ 3.10 Postgres MIT License SQLAlchemy 2 v0.1.0

docs.title: The Problem with LLM-Generated SQL
docs.text:
Every production AI data feature eventually hits the same three walls:

Raw LLM SQL QCE SQL injection via prompt ✗ Never safe ✓ Bind params always Hallucinated table names ✗ Silent wrong answer ✓ Hard error, allowlist enforced Non-deterministic output ✗ Varies per call ✓ Same plan → same SQL LLM picks JOIN strategy ✗ Unpredictable ✓ BFS shortest path, always Needs DB introspection ✗ Exposes full schema ✓ LLM sees logical names only

QCE solves this by separating intent from execution. The LLM produces a structured QueryPlan JSON — no SQL, no table names, no dialect concerns. The compiler takes that plan and produces correct, safe, parameterized SQL every time.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
User question
       │
       ▼
  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  LLM  (OpenAI · Gemini · Groq · any model)  │
  │  Produces: QueryPlan JSON                    │  ← structured intent, not SQL
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────┘
       │
       ▼  { "dataset": "orders", "filters": [...], "metrics": [...] }
  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  QCE Compiler                                │
  │  · Validates against schema.yaml allowlist   │
  │  · All values → bind parameters              │
  │  · Auto-injects JOIN paths via BFS           │
  │  · Resolves $relative_date sentinels         │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────┘
       │
       ▼  SELECT count(*) AS n FROM orders WHERE ship_country = %(p0)s
  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  Postgres                                    │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────┘
       │
       ▼  { "rows": [...], "row_count": 122, "sql": "...", "params": {...} }

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text:
With an LLMHand-written planValidate offline 
from sqlalchemy import create_engine
from openai import OpenAI
import dsl_compiler as qce

engine = create_engine("postgresql+psycopg2://user:pass@host/db")

agent = qce.QueryAgent(
    engine=engine,
    schema_path="config/schema.yaml",
    spec_path="config/queryplan_spec_generated.yaml",
    llm=OpenAI(api_key="sk-..."),
)

result = agent.ask("Top 10 customers by total order value last 90 days")
print(result["rows"])
print(result["sql"])  # always inspect the generated SQL

from sqlalchemy import create_engine
import dsl_compiler as qce

engine = create_engine("postgresql+psycopg2://user:pass@host/db")

result = qce.execute_query_plan(
    engine=engine,
    schema_path="config/schema.yaml",
    query_plan={
        "dataset": "orders",
        "filters": [
            {"field": "ship_country", "op": "=", "value": "Germany"},
            {
                "field": "order_date",
                "op": ">=",
                "value": {"$relative_date": {"op": "now_minus_days", "days": 30}},
            },
        ],
        "metrics": [{"agg": "sum", "field": "freight", "alias": "total_freight"}],
        "limit": 1,
    },
)
print(result["rows"])   # [{"total_freight": 3847.25}]
print(result["sql"])    # SELECT sum(freight) AS total_freight FROM orders
                        # WHERE ship_country = %(p0)s AND order_date >= %(p1)s
                        # LIMIT 1

import dsl_compiler as qce

errors = qce.validate_query_plan(
    query_plan=plan,
    schema_path="config/schema.yaml",
)

if errors:
    for e in errors:
        print(e)   # "$.filters[0].field: unknown column 'revenue' on table 'orders'"
else:
    print("✓ Plan is valid")

docs.title: Feature Highlights
docs.text:
🔒

Injection-Proof by Design

Every filter value becomes a named bind parameter. There is no code path that interpolates user input into SQL text — ever.

📋

Schema Allowlist Enforcement

Only tables and columns declared in schema.yaml are reachable. Unknown names raise a hard QueryPlanError — no silent degradation.

⚙️

Deterministic Output

The compiler is a pure function. Same QueryPlan → same SQL every time. Cache by plan hash, regression-test against a corpus.

🔗

Auto Join Injection

Reference columns from multiple tables. QCE runs BFS over your schema's link graph and injects the shortest join path automatically.

🔄

LLM Retry Loop

Validation errors are structured and fed back to the LLM as focused correction prompts — not stack traces. Models self-correct reliably.

🌐

LLM-Agnostic

One factory function — make_llm_client() — adapts OpenAI, LangChain, Gemini, Groq, Ollama, or any callable automatically.

docs.title: API Reference
docs.text:
All public symbols are importable directly from dsl_compiler:

import dsl_compiler as qce

# or decompose individually:
from dsl_compiler import execute_query_plan, QueryPlanPlanner, QueryAgent

docs.title: Quick Reference
docs.text: Symbol Kind Summary execute_query_plan function Compile + execute a QueryPlan. Primary entrypoint. validate_query_plan function Validate a plan offline. Returns list of error strings. validate_query_plan_dict function Structured validation. Returns typed error objects. load_and_validate_schema function Parse and validate schema.yaml. QueryPlanPlanner class LLM → QueryPlan orchestration with retry loop. QueryAgent class Natural language → executed result in one call. docs.title: execute_query_plan
docs.text:
The primary entrypoint. Compiles and executes a QueryPlan against a live Postgres database.

def execute_query_plan(
    *,
    engine: Engine,
    schema_path: str,
    query_plan: Dict[str, Any],
    raise_on_error: bool = False,
    statement_timeout_ms: int = 30_000,
) -> Dict[str, Any]

Parameters:

Parameter Type Default Description engine sqlalchemy.Engine required A SQLAlchemy engine connected to your Postgres DB schema_path str required Path to your schema.yaml file query_plan dict required A QueryPlan dict (see QueryPlan Reference) raise_on_error bool False If True, raises typed exceptions instead of returning an error dict statement_timeout_ms int 30_000 Per-query timeout in milliseconds

Returns — success:

{
    "rows":      [{"col": val, ...}, ...],  # list of row dicts
    "row_count": 42,                        # number of rows returned
    "columns":   ["col1", "col2", ...],     # column names in order
    "sql":       "SELECT ...",              # compiled SQL (for logging/debug)
    "params":    {"p0": "Germany", ...},    # bind parameter values
    "meta":      {...},                     # forwarded from plan["meta"] if present
}

Returns — error (when raise_on_error=False):

{"error": {"message": "..."}}

Execution steps:

  1. Load and validate schema.yaml
  2. Strip meta key from plan (forwarded to output, not compiled)
  3. Resolve $relative_date sentinels to concrete UTC timestamps
  4. auto_inject_joins — insert shortest join path for multi-table refs
  5. Compiler(schema).compile(plan) → (sql, params)
  6. Executor(engine, timeout).execute(sql, params) → result dict

Example:

from sqlalchemy import create_engine
import dsl_compiler as qce

engine = create_engine("postgresql+psycopg2://user:pass@localhost/mydb")

result = qce.execute_query_plan(
    engine=engine,
    schema_path="config/schema.yaml",
    query_plan={
        "dataset": "orders",
        "filters": [{"field": "ship_country", "op": "=", "value": "Germany"}],
        "metrics": [{"agg": "count", "field": "*", "alias": "n"}],
    },
    raise_on_error=True,
    statement_timeout_ms=10_000,
)
print(result["rows"])

docs.title: validate_query_plan
docs.text:
Validates a QueryPlan offline — no database connection required.

def validate_query_plan(
    query_plan: Dict[str, Any],
    schema_path: str,
) -> List[str]

Returns: A list of error strings. Empty list means valid.

errors = qce.validate_query_plan(plan, "config/schema.yaml")
if errors:
    for e in errors:
        print(e)

Validation runs three checks in order:

  1. Pydantic — structural schema (required fields, valid operators/aggs, extra="forbid")
  2. Allowlist — every table and column present in schema.yaml
  3. Semantic rules — rollup field references inner aliases, metric aliases are unique

docs.title: load_and_validate_schema
docs.text:
Load schema.yaml and run validation.

def load_and_validate_schema(schema_path: str) -> Dict[str, Any]

Returns: Parsed schema dict. Raises: SchemaError on fatal issues. Prints warnings to stdout for non-fatal issues.

schema = qce.load_and_validate_schema("config/schema.yaml")

docs.title: QueryPlanPlanner
docs.text:
LLM → QueryPlan orchestration with validation and retry.

class QueryPlanPlanner:
    def __init__(
        self,
        *,
        llm: Any,
        schema_path: str,
        spec_path: str | None = None,
        spec_dict: dict | None = None,
        temperature: float = 0.0,
        model: str | None = None,
    )

Parameters:

Parameter Description llm Any LLM — OpenAI client, LangChain model, callable, or LLMClient instance schema_path Path to schema.yaml spec_path Path to queryplan_spec_generated.yaml. Auto-generates from schema if omitted. spec_dict Pre-parsed spec dict. Takes priority over spec_path. temperature LLM temperature. Use 0.0 for maximum determinism. model Model name hint (used only by the OpenAI adapter).

docs.title: plan(question) — Single Shot
docs.text:
One LLM call, auto-fixes applied. No validation, no retry. Fastest path.

plan = planner.plan("How many customers are in France?")

docs.title: plan_with_retry(question, max_retries=1) — Full Pipeline
docs.text:
LLM call → auto-fix → validate → semantic lint → retry with structured feedback if needed.

plan = planner.plan_with_retry(
    "Top 5 products by revenue last 30 days",
    max_retries=2,
)

The returned plan includes a meta dict:

plan["meta"] == {
    "plan_hash":          "a3f1b2c9...",  # SHA-256 fingerprint (stable across calls)
    "retry_count":        0,              # 0 = first attempt succeeded
    "auto_fixes_applied": [],             # list of applied auto-fix descriptions
    "validation_errors":  [],             # empty = valid
    "lint_errors":        [],             # semantic lint warnings (non-fatal)
}

docs.title: QueryAgent
docs.text:
High-level wrapper: natural language question → executed result in one call.

class QueryAgent:
    def __init__(
        self,
        *,
        engine: Engine,
        schema_path: str,
        spec_path: str,
        llm: Any,
        max_plan_retries: int = 1,
    )

docs.title: ask(question)
docs.text:
result = agent.ask("What are the top 10 countries by number of orders?")

Returns the same structure as execute_query_plan on success. On plan validation failure after all retries:

{
    "error": {
        "message":          "validation failed after 1 retry",
        "validation_errors": [{"path": "$.metrics[0].field", "message": "..."}],
        "plan":             {...},
    }
}

docs.title: validate_query_plan_dict
docs.text:
Lower-level validation that returns structured error objects.

def validate_query_plan_dict(
    plan_dict: Dict[str, Any],
    schema_path: str,
) -> Tuple[Optional[QueryPlan], List[ValidationErrorItem]]

Returns: (parsed_plan or None, list of ValidationErrorItem)

from dsl_compiler import validate_query_plan_dict

plan, errors = validate_query_plan_dict(plan_dict, "config/schema.yaml")
for e in errors:
    print(f"{e.path}: {e.message}")

ValidationErrorItem fields:

Field Type Description path str JSONPath-style location, e.g. "$.filters[0].field" message str Human-readable error description

docs.title: QueryPlan
docs.text:
The Pydantic model for type-safe plan construction:

from dsl_compiler import QueryPlan

plan = QueryPlan(
    dataset="orders",
    filters=[{"field": "ship_country", "op": "=", "value": "Germany"}],
    metrics=[{"agg": "count", "field": "*", "alias": "n"}],
)

result = qce.execute_query_plan(
    engine=engine,
    schema_path="config/schema.yaml",
    query_plan=plan.model_dump(),
)

docs.title: queryplan_json_schema
docs.text:
Returns the JSON Schema for the QueryPlan model. Pass this to any LLM that supports structured output natively:

from dsl_compiler import queryplan_json_schema

schema = queryplan_json_schema()
# {"type": "object", "properties": {...}, ...}

docs.title: semantic_lint
docs.text:
Run semantic lint checks against a plan without executing it:

def semantic_lint(
    question: str,
    plan: Dict[str, Any],
    schema: Dict[str, Any] | None = None,
) -> List[str]

from dsl_compiler import semantic_lint

errors = semantic_lint(
    question="Top 10 customers by revenue",
    plan=plan_dict,
    schema=schema_dict,  # optional — enables grain checks
)
for e in errors:
    print(e)

docs.title: auto_inject_joins
docs.text:
Lower-level join utilities (auto_inject_joins, build_link_graph, shortest_join_path) for direct pipeline control:

from dsl_compiler import auto_inject_joins, build_link_graph, shortest_join_path

# Build the adjacency graph from schema links
graph = build_link_graph(schema)

# Find shortest path between two tables
path = shortest_join_path(graph, start="order_details", end="customers")
# Returns list of link defs in traversal order, or None if no path

# Auto-inject joins into a plan
enriched_plan = auto_inject_joins(plan_dict, schema)

docs.title: build_spec
docs.text:
Programmatic spec builder (equivalent to the CLI command):

from dsl_compiler import build_spec, write_spec

spec = build_spec("config/schema.yaml")          # returns dict
write_spec(spec, "config/queryplan_spec_generated.yaml")

docs.title: get_queryplan_instructions
docs.text:
Build the full LLM system prompt string from spec + schema:

from dsl_compiler.api.spec_api import get_queryplan_instructions

prompt = get_queryplan_instructions(
    schema_path="config/schema.yaml",
    spec_path="config/queryplan_spec_generated.yaml",
    include_schema_yaml=True,   # append raw schema.yaml — recommended
)

Useful when building your own message lists instead of using QueryPlanPlanner.

docs.title: Compiler (low-level)
docs.text:
Direct access to the compiler if you manage the pipeline yourself:

from dsl_compiler.compiler import Compiler

schema = qce.load_and_validate_schema("config/schema.yaml")
compiler = Compiler(
    schema,
    default_limit=100,
    max_limit=1000,
    max_joins=8,
)

sql, params = compiler.compile(plan_dict)
print(sql)
print(params)

compile() returns (sql_string, params_dict) using named bind parameters compatible with psycopg2 (%(name)s style).

docs.title: Core Concepts
docs.text:
Understanding how QCE works end-to-end will help you configure it correctly and debug issues when they arise.

TL;DR

QCE separates intent (what data you want) from execution (how to fetch it). The LLM produces a structured QueryPlan; the compiler turns that into parameterized SQL. The two never mix.

docs.title: The Compilation Pipeline
docs.text:
Every QCE query goes through the same deterministic stages:

Natural language question
        │
        ▼  ── optional: skip if you write plans by hand ──
 ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
 │  LLM Planner                                         │
 │  QueryPlanPlanner.plan_with_retry()                  │
 │                                                      │
 │  • sends: question + schema + spec YAML              │
 │  • receives: QueryPlan JSON dict                     │
 │  • applies: auto-fixes (relative dates, limits...)   │
 │  • validates & retries with structured error feedback │
 └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
        │
        ▼  QueryPlan dict  ── pure JSON, zero SQL ──
 ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
 │  Validation   validate_query_plan_dict()             │
 │                                                      │
 │  1. Pydantic — structural check (extra="forbid")     │
 │  2. Allowlist — every table/column in schema.yaml    │
 │  3. Semantic — rollup integrity, alias uniqueness    │
 └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
        │
        ▼
 ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
 │  Auto Join Inject   auto_inject_joins()              │
 │                                                      │
 │  BFS over schema links → inject shortest join path  │
 └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
        │
        ▼
 ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
 │  Compiler   Compiler.compile()                       │
 │                                                      │
 │  • logical names → physical names (schema.yaml)      │
 │  • all values → %(p0)s bind parameters               │
 │  • builds SQLAlchemy expression tree                 │
 │  • renders: parameterized SQL string                 │
 └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
        │
        ▼  (sql_string, params_dict)
 ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
 │  Executor   Executor.execute()                       │
 │                                                      │
 │  • SET LOCAL statement_timeout first                 │
 │  • executes with bind params                         │
 │  • serializes: datetime → ISO, Decimal → float      │
 └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
        │
        ▼
   {"rows": [...], "row_count": N, "columns": [...], "sql": "..."}

docs.title: The Schema: Your Allowlist
docs.text:
schema.yaml is the single source of truth for everything the compiler is allowed to touch.

Security guarantee

If a table or column is not declared in schema.yaml, it cannot appear in compiled SQL — not from the LLM, not from a hand-written plan, not from user input of any kind. The compiler raises QueryPlanError and execution never happens.

The schema defines three things:

Tables & ColumnsLinks (Joins)Primary Keys 
tables:
  orders:                    # logical name — the LLM uses this
    db_table: "Orders"       # physical name — the DB sees this
    description: "Customer orders"
    columns:
      order_id:
        db_column: "OrderID"
        type: integer
      freight:
        db_column: "Freight"
        type: float

links:
  - name: orders_to_customers
    from_table: orders
    from_column: customer_id
    to_table: customers
    to_column: customer_id
    join_type: LEFT

Links are used by the auto-join injector to find join paths automatically.

tables:
  orders:
    primary_key: order_id

Primary keys help the semantic linter suggest count_distinct over count when appropriate.

docs.title: The QueryPlan: Structured Intent
docs.text:
A QueryPlan expresses what data to fetch, not how to fetch it as SQL. The LLM produces this structure; the compiler handles all SQL decisions.

{
  "dataset": "orders",
  "filters": [{"field": "ship_country", "op": "=", "value": "Germany"}],
  "metrics": [{"agg": "count", "field": "*", "alias": "total_orders"}]
}

Compiles to:

SELECT count(*) AS total_orders
FROM "Orders"
WHERE "ShipCountry" = %(p0)s

No SQL in the plan

The plan has no FROM, WHERE, GROUP BY, or SQL keywords — those are the compiler's responsibility. The LLM only declares intent.

See the QueryPlan Reference for the full spec.

docs.title: Determinism
docs.text:
The compiler is a pure function: identical input always produces identical output.

  • No randomness in compilation (LLM temperature is isolated to the planning stage)
  • Bind params (p0, p1, …) are generated from a reset counter on each call
  • SHA-256 plan_hash attached to every generated plan

This enables cache-by-hash patterns:

plan = planner.plan_with_retry(question)
cache_key = plan["meta"]["plan_hash"]

if cache_key not in result_cache:
    result_cache[cache_key] = qce.execute_query_plan(
        engine=engine, schema_path=..., query_plan=plan
    )
return result_cache[cache_key]

docs.title: Parameterized Values
docs.text:
Every filter value becomes a named bind parameter — it is never string-interpolated into SQL:

plan = {"dataset": "orders", "filters": [{"field": "freight", "op": ">", "value": 100}]}
sql, params = Compiler(schema).compile(plan)
# sql:    "SELECT ... FROM orders WHERE freight > %(p0)s"
# params: {"p0": 100}

This is identical to what cursor.execute(sql, params) requires. There is no mechanism by which a filter value can escape into SQL syntax, making SQL injection structurally impossible regardless of what the LLM (or a user) provides.

docs.title: Rollup: Two-Level Aggregation
docs.text:
Some questions require aggregating an already-aggregated result — for example: "What is the average order value per customer?"

This needs two layers:

  1. Inner query — sum order value per customer
  2. Outer query — average those sums

QCE models this with rollup:

{
  "dataset": "order_details",
  "dimensions": [{"field": "order_details.order_id"}],
  "metrics": [{"agg": "sum", "field": "order_details.unit_price", "alias": "revenue"}],
  "rollup": {
    "metrics": [{"agg": "avg", "field": "revenue", "alias": "avg_revenue"}],
    "limit": 1
  }
}

The compiler wraps the inner query in a CTE and applies the outer aggregation over it. The rollup.field values must match aliases from the inner metrics — the semantic validator enforces this.

docs.title: Auto Join Injection
docs.text:
When a plan references columns from multiple tables, QCE automatically computes and injects the join path:

{
  "dataset": "orders",
  "dimensions": [
    {"field": "orders.order_date"},
    {"field": "customers.country"}
  ],
  "metrics": [{"agg": "count", "field": "*", "alias": "n"}]
}

QCE performs BFS over the links graph in schema.yaml, finds the shortest path from orders to customers, and injects the join definition into the plan — no manual join declaration needed.

Explicit joins override auto-injection

If you declare joins in the plan's joins array, auto-injection is skipped for that plan. Use explicit joins to control join type (INNER vs LEFT) or when the BFS path is ambiguous.

docs.title: Semantic Lint
docs.text:
Before retrying with the LLM, semantic_lint compares the user's question against the plan and flags common model mistakes:

Question signal Expected plan property Rule "distinct values", "unique" count_distinct not count 1 "per X", "by X", "each X" non-empty dimensions 2 "top N", "most", "highest" order_by + limit set 3 "average per", "avg per" rollup present 4 "how many", "count of" count_distinct(primary_key) 5

Lint errors are appended to the structured retry message sent back to the LLM, giving it an explicit chance to self-correct.

docs.title: The Spec Builder
docs.text:
The spec builder auto-generates the LLM's system prompt from your schema.yaml:

python -m dsl_compiler.spec_builder \
  --schema config/schema.yaml \
  --output config/queryplan_spec_generated.yaml

The generated spec contains:

  • System instructions (produce JSON only, never SQL, use logical names)
  • Schema summary (tables, columns, types, descriptions)
  • The exact QueryPlan JSON shape with field descriptions
  • Operator and aggregation reference
  • Concrete examples auto-derived from your schema

Regenerate after every schema change

The spec is the LLM's only knowledge of your database structure. If you add a table and don't regenerate, the LLM cannot reference it — and the compiler will reject any column it doesn't know about regardless.

docs.title: Contributing
docs.text:
Thanks for your interest in contributing to QCE!

New to the codebase?

Read Core Concepts first to understand the pipeline, then come back here. The best first contribution is usually a new operator or aggregation — both require only a few targeted edits.

docs.title: 1. Clone the Repository
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/alexanderabakah/dsl_compiler
cd dsl_compiler

docs.title: 2. Create a Virtual Environment
docs.text:
python3 -m venv env_dsl
source env_dsl/bin/activate   # Linux/macOS
# or: env_dsl\Scripts\activate  # Windows

docs.title: 3. Install in Editable Mode
docs.text:
pip install -e ".[dev]"

This installs dsl_compiler/qce in editable mode (source changes are reflected immediately), plus pytest, ruff, build, and twine.

docs.title: 4. Install LLM Extras (optional)
docs.text:
pip install -e ".[dev,openai]"          # OpenAI SDK
pip install langchain-google-genai      # Gemini via LangChain
pip install langchain-groq              # Groq via LangChain
pip install langchain-ollama            # Ollama (local models)

docs.title: Project Structure
docs.text:
dsl_compiler/
  __init__.py           # public API — everything exported from here
  compiler.py           # core SQL compiler: logical → physical, values → bind params
  executor.py           # executes compiled SQL against Postgres
  planner.py            # LLM → QueryPlan orchestration + retry loop
  validation.py         # three-layer validation: Pydantic + allowlist + semantic
  join_planner.py       # BFS auto-join injection
  semantic_lint.py      # question-aware lint checks (5 rules)
  spec_builder.py       # auto-generates LLM prompt spec from schema.yaml
  queryplan_models.py   # Pydantic models for the QueryPlan DSL
  llm_client.py         # LLMClient protocol + CallableLLMClient
  llm_adapters.py       # OpenAI and LangChain adapters + make_llm_client() factory
  exceptions.py         # structured exception hierarchy
  agent.py              # high-level QueryAgent (planner + executor combined)
  api/
    api.py              # execute_query_plan, validate_query_plan, load_and_validate_schema
    spec_api.py         # get_queryplan_instructions

config/
  schema.yaml                     # demo Northwind schema
  queryplan_spec.yaml             # hand-authored reference spec
  queryplan_spec_generated.yaml   # auto-generated — regenerate after schema changes

test/
  test_main.py          # unit and integration tests
  regression_test/      # 28-question Northwind regression suite

benchmark/              # benchmarks: QCE vs LangChain vs GPT-4 Direct
docs/                   # this documentation site (MkDocs)

docs.title: Running Tests
docs.text: pytest test/ -v

docs.title: Regression Suite
docs.text:
The regression suite (test/regression_test/) runs 28 real queries against a Northwind Postgres database. It requires database credentials:

cp test/.env.example test/.env
# fill in: DB_HOST, DB_PORT, DB_NAME, DB_USER, DB_PASSWORD

pytest test/regression_test/ -v

docs.title: Linting and Formatting
docs.text:
QCE uses Ruff:

ruff check dsl_compiler/         # check for issues
ruff check --fix dsl_compiler/   # auto-fix
ruff format dsl_compiler/        # format

All configuration lives in pyproject.toml.

docs.title: Regenerating the Spec
docs.text:
After any change to schema.yaml:

python -m dsl_compiler.spec_builder \
    --schema config/schema.yaml \
    --output config/queryplan_spec_generated.yaml

docs.title: Common Extension Patterns
docs.text:
New OperatorNew AggregationNew Semantic Lint Rule
  1. Add the literal to Operator in queryplan_models.py
  2. Handle it in Compiler._compile_bool_expr in compiler.py
  3. Add it to _operators_block() in spec_builder.py
  4. Add a test case in test/test_main.py
  1. Add the literal to Agg in queryplan_models.py
  2. Handle it in Compiler._build_select_query in compiler.py
  3. Add a test case in test/test_main.py
  1. Add a _check_* function in semantic_lint.py
  2. Call it from semantic_lint()
  3. Add a test that provides a question with the pattern and a plan that violates the rule

docs.title: Submitting a Pull Request
docs.text:
1. Fork and create a feature branch:

    git checkout -b feat/my-feature

  2. Write tests for your changes — all PRs require coverage for new behaviour.

  3. Run checks:

    ruff check dsl_compiler/ && pytest test/ -v

  4. Commit with a clear message:

    git commit -m "feat: add support for BETWEEN operator in compiler"

  5. Open a PR against main. Describe what the change does and why.

docs.text:
python -m build
# output: dist/

python -m twine upload dist/*

Requires a PyPI API token set as TWINE_PASSWORD or in ~/.pypirc.

docs.title: Docs Locally
docs.text:
pip install -r requirements-docs.txt
mkdocs serve --dev-addr 127.0.0.1:8001

Open http://127.0.0.1:8001 to preview.

mkdocs build   # static HTML → site/

docs.title: Code Style Guidelines
docs.text:
• Type annotations on all public functions and constructors
  • Keep functions focused — the compiler's _compile_* methods are a good model
  • Raise typed exceptions (QueryPlanError, SchemaError, …) — never bare Exception from public paths
  • All filter values must go through bind parameters — never str.format() or f-strings into SQL
  • Tests should be self-contained and not require a live DB unless they're in regression_test/

docs.title: Exception Reference
docs.text:
QCE uses a structured exception hierarchy. All exceptions inherit from DSLCompilerError, making it easy to catch all library errors in one place or handle each type separately.

from dsl_compiler import (
    DSLCompilerError,
    SchemaError,
    QueryPlanError,
    AmbiguousColumnError,
    DatabaseExecutionError,
    QueryCostError,
)

docs.title: Hierarchy
docs.text:
Exception
└── DSLCompilerError
    ├── SchemaError              ← bad schema.yaml configuration
    ├── QueryPlanError           ← invalid or unresolvable query plan
    │   └── AmbiguousColumnError ← unqualified column matches multiple tables
    ├── DatabaseExecutionError   ← valid SQL, but Postgres rejected it
    └── QueryCostError           ← reserved for future cost-guard use

docs.title: DSLCompilerError
docs.text:
Base class for all QCE exceptions. Catch this to handle any library error in one place:

try:
    result = qce.execute_query_plan(...)
except qce.DSLCompilerError as e:
    print(f"QCE error: {e}")

docs.title: SchemaError
docs.text:
Raised when schema.yaml is missing, malformed, or contains invalid configuration.

When you'll see it:

  • schema.yaml does not exist or is not valid YAML
  • A table entry is missing name, db_table, or columns
  • A column entry is missing name or db_column
  • A link references a table not present in tables
  • A link's "on" field was parsed as a boolean (YAML interprets bare on: as true)
try:
    schema = qce.load_and_validate_schema("config/schema.yaml")
except qce.SchemaError as e:
    print(f"Fix your schema.yaml: {e}")

Common YAML pitfall: bare on: key

YAML parses on as boolean true unless quoted. Always quote it:

# ❌ "on" parsed as boolean true — causes SchemaError
links:
  - name: orders_to_customers
    on:
      - from_col: customer_id

# ✅ correct
links:
  - name: orders_to_customers
    "on":
      - from_col: customer_id

docs.title: QueryPlanError
docs.text:
Raised when a QueryPlan is structurally or semantically invalid. This is the most common exception during development.

Constructor:

QueryPlanError(
    message: str,
    *,
    code: str = "INVALID_PLAN",
    path: str = "$",
    suggestion: str | None = None,
    validation_errors: list | None = None,
)

Attributes:

Attribute Type Description message str Human-readable error description code str Machine-readable error code path str JSONPath error location, e.g. "$.filters[0].field" suggestion str \| None Suggested fix (when available) validation_errors list \| None Structured validation error list

Method:

error.to_dict()
# {"code": "...", "message": "...", "path": "...", "suggestion": "...", "validation_errors": [...]}

When you'll see it:

  • dataset references a table not in schema.yaml
  • A field references a column not in schema.yaml
  • A rollup.metrics[*].field references a raw column instead of an inner metric alias
  • Metric aliases are not unique
  • Unknown fields are present in the plan (extra="forbid")
  • agg requires a field but none was provided
try:
    sql, params = Compiler(schema).compile({"dataset": "nonexistent", ...})
except qce.QueryPlanError as e:
    print(e.code)       # "INVALID_PLAN"
    print(e.path)       # "$.dataset"
    print(e.message)    # "Unknown dataset: nonexistent"

Designed for LLM retry feedback

QueryPlanError is serializable by design. When using plan_with_retry, QCE feeds the error dict back to the LLM automatically. In a custom retry loop:

try:
    sql, params = compiler.compile(plan)
except qce.QueryPlanError as e:
    error_context = e.to_dict()
    # Append error_context to your next LLM message

docs.title: AmbiguousColumnError
docs.text:
A subclass of QueryPlanError raised when an unqualified column reference matches columns in multiple joined tables.

Constructor:

AmbiguousColumnError(column: str, tables: List[str], path: str = "$")

Attributes:

Attribute Type Description column str The ambiguous column name tables list[str] All tables that contain this column

Example: When orders is joined with customers and both have customer_id:

{
  "dataset": "orders",
  "dimensions": [{"field": "customer_id"}]
}

This raises AmbiguousColumnError. Fix: qualify the reference:

{"field": "orders.customer_id"}

docs.title: DatabaseExecutionError
docs.text:
Raised when a valid, compiled query fails at the Postgres level.

Constructor:

DatabaseExecutionError(
    message: str,
    *,
    sql: str | None = None,
    original: Exception | None = None,
)

Attributes:

Attribute Type Description sql str \| None The SQL that triggered the error original Exception \| None The underlying psycopg2/SQLAlchemy exception

When you'll see it:

  • Statement timeout exceeded (statement_timeout_ms)
  • Postgres connection failure
  • Permission denied on a table
  • Type mismatch between a filter value and the column type
try:
    result = qce.execute_query_plan(engine=engine, ..., raise_on_error=True)
except qce.DatabaseExecutionError as e:
    print(f"DB error: {e.message}")
    print(f"SQL: {e.sql}")
    print(f"Caused by: {e.original}")

docs.title: QueryCostError
docs.text:
Raised when a query exceeds a configured complexity threshold before execution.

Constructor:

QueryCostError(message: str, *, estimated_cost: float | None = None)

Reserved for future use

QueryCostError is defined in the exception hierarchy but is not currently raised by compiler internals. It is reserved for a future cost-based query guard feature.

docs.title: Production Error Handling Pattern
docs.text:
Recommended pattern for a web API endpoint using raise_on_error=True:

import dsl_compiler as qce

def handle_question(question: str) -> dict:
    try:
        plan = planner.plan_with_retry(question)
        return qce.execute_query_plan(
            engine=engine,
            schema_path="config/schema.yaml",
            query_plan=plan,
            raise_on_error=True,
        )

    except qce.QueryPlanError as e:
        # LLM generated an invalid plan; all retries exhausted
        # Safe to surface the error code to the client
        return {"error": "invalid_query", "detail": e.message, "code": e.code}

    except qce.DatabaseExecutionError as e:
        # Valid SQL but DB rejected it (timeout, permissions, type error)
        # Log e.sql for debugging; do NOT surface raw SQL to end users
        logger.error("DB execution failed", sql=e.sql, cause=str(e.original))
        return {"error": "execution_failed", "detail": "Query failed. Please try again."}

    except qce.SchemaError as e:
        # Configuration error — should never reach production if schema is tested at startup
        logger.critical("Schema configuration error", error=str(e))
        return {"error": "configuration_error", "detail": "Internal error"}

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text: Get from zero to a running QCE query in under five minutes.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Python ≥ 3.10
  • A running Postgres database (local or remote)
  • Optional: an LLM API key (OpenAI, Gemini, Groq, etc.) — only needed if you want natural language input

docs.title: 1. Install
docs.text:
pip install qce

Install with LLM extras if you plan to use natural language input:

pip install "qce[openai]"                   # OpenAI SDK
pip install langchain-openai                # LangChain + OpenAI
pip install langchain-google-genai          # Gemini (free tier available)
pip install langchain-groq                  # Groq (free, fast)
Install from source 
git clone https://github.com/alexanderabakah/dsl_compiler
cd dsl_compiler
pip install -e ".[dev]"

docs.title: 2. Define Your Schema
docs.text:
schema.yaml is the allowlist that tells QCE which tables and columns exist. The LLM only sees logical names; the compiler maps them to physical Postgres identifiers.

Create config/schema.yaml:

version: 1
dialect: postgres

tables:
  - name: orders           # (1)
    db_table: orders       # (2)
    description: "Customer orders"
    primary_id: order_id   # (3)
    columns:
      - { name: order_id,    db_column: order_id,    type: integer }
      - { name: customer_id, db_column: customer_id, type: varchar }
      - { name: order_date,  db_column: order_date,  type: date    }
      - { name: freight,     db_column: freight,     type: numeric }
      - { name: ship_country,db_column: ship_country,type: varchar }

  - name: customers
    db_table: customers
    primary_id: customer_id
    columns:
      - { name: customer_id, db_column: customer_id, type: varchar }
      - { name: company_name,db_column: company_name,type: varchar }
      - { name: country,     db_column: country,     type: varchar }

links:
  - name: orders_to_customers
    from_table: orders
    to_table: customers
    join_type: left
    "on": # (4)
      - { from_col: customer_id, to_col: customer_id }
  1. Logical name — what appears in QueryPlan dataset and field refs
  2. Physical name — the actual Postgres table name
  3. Primary key — used by semantic lint to suggest count_distinct on the right column
  4. Quote "on": — on is a YAML reserved word; always quote it

Always quote \"on\":

Forgetting the quotes causes the YAML parser to read on: [...] as true: [...], which QCE catches with a clear SchemaError — but save yourself the confusion and quote it from the start.

See the full Schema Reference for all options.

docs.title: 3. Generate the LLM Spec
docs.text:
QCE auto-generates a compact prompt file from your schema. Run this once, then regenerate whenever schema.yaml changes:

python -m dsl_compiler.spec_builder \
    --schema config/schema.yaml \
    --output config/queryplan_spec_generated.yaml

This creates the system prompt that tells the LLM what tables and columns exist, what the QueryPlan shape looks like, and how to construct valid plans. You never have to write it by hand.

docs.title: Option A — Hand-written plan (no LLM needed)
docs.text:
quickstart.py
from sqlalchemy import create_engine
import dsl_compiler as qce

engine = create_engine("postgresql+psycopg2://user:pass@localhost/mydb")

result = qce.execute_query_plan(
    engine=engine,
    schema_path="config/schema.yaml",
    query_plan={
        "dataset": "orders",
        "filters": [
            {"field": "ship_country", "op": "=", "value": "Germany"}
        ],
        "metrics": [
            {"agg": "count", "field": "*", "alias": "total_orders"},
            {"agg": "sum",   "field": "freight", "alias": "total_freight"},
        ],
        "limit": 1,
    },
)

print(result["rows"])
# [{"total_orders": 122, "total_freight": 4891.5}]

print(result["sql"])
# SELECT count(*) AS total_orders, sum(freight) AS total_freight
# FROM orders WHERE ship_country = %(p0)s

docs.title: Option B — Natural language via QueryAgent
docs.text:
agent_quickstart.py
from sqlalchemy import create_engine
from openai import OpenAI
import dsl_compiler as qce

engine = create_engine("postgresql+psycopg2://user:pass@localhost/mydb")

agent = qce.QueryAgent(
    engine=engine,
    schema_path="config/schema.yaml",
    spec_path="config/queryplan_spec_generated.yaml",
    llm=OpenAI(api_key="sk-..."),
    max_plan_retries=1,
)

result = agent.ask("Which country had the most orders last month?")
print(result["rows"])    # [{"ship_country": "Germany", "n": 34}]
print(result["sql"])

docs.title: Option C — LLM planner, execute yourself
docs.text:
planner_quickstart.py
from sqlalchemy import create_engine
from openai import OpenAI
import dsl_compiler as qce

engine = create_engine("postgresql+psycopg2://user:pass@localhost/mydb")

planner = qce.QueryPlanPlanner(
    llm=OpenAI(api_key="sk-..."),
    schema_path="config/schema.yaml",
    spec_path="config/queryplan_spec_generated.yaml",
)

# Step 1: generate a QueryPlan from natural language
plan = planner.plan_with_retry("Top 5 customers by freight spend", max_retries=2)
print(plan["meta"]["retry_count"])       # 0 — succeeded on first attempt
print(plan["meta"]["auto_fixes_applied"])

# Step 2: validate independently (optional — execute_query_plan does this too)
errors = qce.validate_query_plan(plan, "config/schema.yaml")
assert not errors

# Step 3: execute
result = qce.execute_query_plan(engine=engine, schema_path="config/schema.yaml", query_plan=plan)
print(result["rows"])

docs.title: 5. Validate Without a Database
docs.text:
Use validate_query_plan to check plans in unit tests, CI, or a plan preview endpoint — no database connection required:

errors = qce.validate_query_plan(
    query_plan=plan,
    schema_path="config/schema.yaml",
)

if errors:
    for e in errors:
        print(e)
    # "$.filters[0].field: unknown column 'revenue' on table 'orders'"
else:
    print("✓ Plan is valid")

docs.title: 1. Installation
docs.text:
pip install qce

Optional extras — install only what you need for your LLM stack:

pip install "qce[openai]"    # adds openai>=1.0.0
pip install "qce[google]"    # adds google-generativeai>=0.5.0
pip install "qce[dev]"       # adds pytest, ruff, build, twine

Installing from source

git clone https://github.com/alexanderabakah/dsl_compiler
cd dsl_compiler
pip install -e ".[dev]"

docs.title: 2. Define Your Schema
docs.text:
QCE needs a schema.yaml file that maps your database's physical tables and columns to logical names the LLM will use. This file acts as the compiler allowlist — nothing outside it is ever accessible.

Create config/schema.yaml:

version: 1
dialect: postgres

tables:
  - name: orders           # logical name (what the LLM uses)
    db_table: orders       # physical table name in Postgres
    description: "Customer orders"
    primary_id: order_id   # used for count_distinct grain checks
    columns:
      - name: order_id
        db_column: order_id
        type: integer
      - name: customer_id
        db_column: customer_id
        type: varchar
      - name: order_date
        db_column: order_date
        type: date
      - name: freight
        db_column: freight
        type: numeric
        description: "Shipping freight cost"
      - name: ship_country
        db_column: ship_country
        type: varchar

  - name: customers
    db_table: customers
    description: "Customer master"
    primary_id: customer_id
    columns:
      - name: customer_id
        db_column: customer_id
        type: varchar
      - name: company_name
        db_column: company_name
        type: varchar
      - name: country
        db_column: country
        type: varchar

# Join paths between tables
links:
  - name: orders_to_customers
    from_table: orders
    to_table: customers
    join_type: left
    "on":                            # must be quoted — "on" is a reserved word in YAML
      - from_col: customer_id
        to_col: customer_id

on: must be quoted

on is a reserved boolean in YAML 1.1. Always write "on": (with quotes) in your links section, otherwise the YAML parser interprets it as true.

See the Schema Reference for the full format.

docs.title: 3. Generate the LLM Spec
docs.text:
QCE auto-generates a compact LLM instruction file from your schema. This file tells the LLM what tables and columns exist and how to structure a valid QueryPlan.

python -m dsl_compiler.spec_builder \
    --schema config/schema.yaml \
    --output config/queryplan_spec_generated.yaml

Regenerate this file whenever schema.yaml changes.

docs.title: Without an LLM — hand-written plan
docs.text:
from sqlalchemy import create_engine
import dsl_compiler as qce

engine = create_engine("postgresql+psycopg2://user:pass@localhost/mydb")

plan = {
    "dataset": "orders",
    "filters": [
        {"field": "ship_country", "op": "=", "value": "Germany"}
    ],
    "metrics": [
        {"agg": "count", "field": "*", "alias": "total_orders"}
    ],
}

result = qce.execute_query_plan(
    engine=engine,
    schema_path="config/schema.yaml",
    query_plan=plan,
)

print(result["rows"])       # [{"total_orders": 122}]
print(result["sql"])        # SELECT count(*) AS total_orders FROM orders WHERE ...
print(result["row_count"])  # 1

docs.title: With an LLM — natural language → QueryPlan → SQL
docs.text:
from sqlalchemy import create_engine
from openai import OpenAI
import dsl_compiler as qce

engine = create_engine("postgresql+psycopg2://user:pass@localhost/mydb")
openai_client = OpenAI(api_key="sk-...")

agent = qce.QueryAgent(
    engine=engine,
    schema_path="config/schema.yaml",
    spec_path="config/queryplan_spec_generated.yaml",
    llm=openai_client,
)

result = agent.ask("How many orders were shipped to Germany?")
print(result["rows"])

docs.title: 5. Validate Without Executing
docs.text:
To check a plan is valid without a database connection:

import dsl_compiler as qce

errors = qce.validate_query_plan(
    query_plan=plan,
    schema_path="config/schema.yaml",
)

if errors:
    for e in errors:
        print(e)
else:
    print("Plan is valid")

docs.title: 6. What's Next?
docs.text: Topic Description Core Concepts Understand the full compilation pipeline QueryPlan Reference Filters, aggregations, rollup, relative dates LLM Integration OpenAI, LangChain, Gemini, Groq, custom callable Schema Reference Full schema.yaml format API Reference All public functions and classes

docs.title: LLM Integration
docs.text: QCE is LLM-agnostic. One factory function — make_llm_client() — adapts any model object automatically. You are never locked into a specific provider.

docs.title: How Adapter Detection Works
docs.text:
When you pass an LLM object to QueryPlanPlanner or QueryAgent, QCE calls make_llm_client(obj) internally and selects the right adapter:

make_llm_client(obj)
      │
      ├─ has generate_json()  →  use as-is      (already an LLMClient)
      │
      ├─ has .responses.create  →  OpenAIResponsesJSONAdapter
      │                             (OpenAI Responses API, structured output)
      │
      ├─ has .invoke()  →  LangChainJSONAdapter
      │                     (any LangChain chat model)
      │
      └─ is callable  →  CallableLLMClient
                          (your own function)

You can also import and use adapters directly for fine-grained control.

docs.title: Supported Providers
docs.text:
OpenAIGoogle Gemini (Free)Groq (Free + Fast)LangChain (any model)Ollama (Fully Local)Custom Callable

Best for: Production workloads. gpt-4o-mini offers the best quality/cost ratio.

pip install "qce[openai]"

from openai import OpenAI
import dsl_compiler as qce

planner = qce.QueryPlanPlanner(
    llm=OpenAI(api_key="sk-..."),
    schema_path="config/schema.yaml",
    spec_path="config/queryplan_spec_generated.yaml",
)

The OpenAI adapter uses the Responses API with structured output (response_format=json_schema), which guarantees the model returns valid JSON matching the QueryPlan schema. Requires gpt-4o-mini or newer.

Specify a different model:

from dsl_compiler.llm_adapters import OpenAIResponsesJSONAdapter

adapter = OpenAIResponsesJSONAdapter(OpenAI(api_key="sk-..."), model="gpt-4o")
planner = qce.QueryPlanPlanner(llm=adapter, ...)

Best for: Development and testing. Free tier: 1,500 req/day, no credit card.

Get a free API key at aistudio.google.com.

pip install langchain-google-genai

from langchain_google_genai import ChatGoogleGenerativeAI
import dsl_compiler as qce

planner = qce.QueryPlanPlanner(
    llm=ChatGoogleGenerativeAI(
        model="gemini-2.0-flash",
        google_api_key="AIza...",
        temperature=0,
    ),
    schema_path="config/schema.yaml",
    spec_path="config/queryplan_spec_generated.yaml",
)

Best for: Low-latency development. Free tier, no credit card required.

Get a free API key at console.groq.com.

pip install langchain-groq

from langchain_groq import ChatGroq
import dsl_compiler as qce

planner = qce.QueryPlanPlanner(
    llm=ChatGroq(
        model="llama-3.3-70b-versatile",
        api_key="gsk_...",
        temperature=0,
    ),
    schema_path="config/schema.yaml",
    spec_path="config/queryplan_spec_generated.yaml",
)

Any langchain_core chat model with .invoke() works:

pip install langchain-openai   # or langchain-anthropic, etc.

from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI
import dsl_compiler as qce

planner = qce.QueryPlanPlanner(
    llm=ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o-mini", temperature=0, api_key="sk-..."),
    schema_path="config/schema.yaml",
    spec_path="config/queryplan_spec_generated.yaml",
)

The LangChain adapter parses JSON from the model's text output directly — no tool calling or structured output required — so it works with any model, including open-source ones.

Best for: Air-gapped environments, privacy requirements, iterating without API costs.

# Install Ollama from https://ollama.ai, then pull a model
ollama pull qwen2.5:7b
pip install langchain-ollama

from langchain_ollama import ChatOllama
import dsl_compiler as qce

planner = qce.QueryPlanPlanner(
    llm=ChatOllama(model="qwen2.5:7b"),
    schema_path="config/schema.yaml",
    spec_path="config/queryplan_spec_generated.yaml",
)

Model recommendations for local use

qwen2.5:7b and mistral:7b follow JSON instructions reliably. llama3.2:3b is faster but may need max_retries=2.

Pass any function that matches this signature:

def my_llm(
    json_schema: dict,
    messages: list[dict],
    temperature: float,
) -> dict:
    ...

import anthropic
import json
import dsl_compiler as qce

client = anthropic.Anthropic(api_key="sk-ant-...")

def claude_generate(json_schema, messages, temperature):
    system = next((m["content"] for m in messages if m["role"] == "system"), "")
    user   = next((m["content"] for m in messages if m["role"] == "user"),   "")
    resp = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-3-5-haiku-latest",
        max_tokens=2048,
        system=system + f"\n\nReturn ONLY JSON matching this schema:\n{json.dumps(json_schema)}",
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": user}],
    )
    return json.loads(resp.content[0].text)

planner = qce.QueryPlanPlanner(
    llm=claude_generate,
    schema_path="config/schema.yaml",
    spec_path="config/queryplan_spec_generated.yaml",
)

docs.title: QueryPlanPlanner
docs.text:
QueryPlanPlanner handles the full LLM → QueryPlan cycle:

planner = qce.QueryPlanPlanner(
    llm=your_llm,
    schema_path="config/schema.yaml",
    spec_path="config/queryplan_spec_generated.yaml",
    temperature=0.0,         # use 0 for determinism
)

docs.title: plan() — Single Shot
docs.text:
One LLM call, auto-fixes applied, no validation, no retry. Fastest path.

plan = planner.plan("How many orders shipped to Germany last week?")

Use this when you handle validation yourself, or in a streaming context.

docs.title: plan_with_retry() — Full Pipeline
docs.text:
The production path. Validates the plan and feeds structured error feedback back to the LLM if needed.

plan = planner.plan_with_retry(
    "Average freight cost per destination country, top 10",
    max_retries=2,  # default: 1
)

The plan includes a meta dict with full observability:

print(plan["meta"])
# {
#   "plan_hash":            "a3f1b2c9d4e5",  # deterministic SHA-256 fingerprint
#   "retry_count":          0,               # 0 = succeeded on first attempt
#   "auto_fixes_applied":   [],              # e.g. ["forced limit=1 for scalar aggregate"]
#   "validation_errors":    [],              # empty = valid
#   "lint_errors":          [],              # semantic lint warnings (non-fatal)
# }

docs.title: Auto-Fixes Applied Before Validation
docs.text: Condition Fix applied Filter value is a $relative_date sentinel Resolved to concrete UTC timestamp No dimensions + only scalar aggregations limit=1, offset=0 forced Grouped plan with rollup, no top-N intent limit removed from inner plan Columns from multiple tables, no joins declared Shortest BFS join path injected

docs.title: QueryAgent
docs.text:
The highest-level API — combines planner + executor in one call:

agent = qce.QueryAgent(
    engine=engine,
    schema_path="config/schema.yaml",
    spec_path="config/queryplan_spec_generated.yaml",
    llm=your_llm,
    max_plan_retries=1,
)

result = agent.ask("What are the top 5 products by revenue this quarter?")

Success response:

{
    "rows":      [{"product_name": "...", "revenue": 12345.67}, ...],
    "row_count": 5,
    "columns":   ["product_name", "revenue"],
    "sql":       "SELECT product_name, sum(...) ...",
    "params":    {"p0": "2026-01-01", ...},
    "meta":      {"plan_hash": "...", "retry_count": 0, ...},
}

Error response (plan failed validation after all retries):

{
    "error": {
        "message": "validation failed after 1 retry",
        "validation_errors": [
            {"path": "$.metrics[0].field", "message": "unknown column 'revenue' on 'orders'"}
        ],
        "plan": {...},
    }
}

docs.title: What the LLM Receives
docs.text:
QCE sends the LLM exactly three messages:

[system]  QCE system instructions
          "Produce ONLY valid JSON. Never write SQL. Use logical names only..."

[system]  Your spec YAML + schema YAML
          (tables, columns, operators, QueryPlan shape, examples)

[user]    The natural language question

To inspect the exact prompt:

from dsl_compiler.api.spec_api import get_queryplan_instructions

prompt = get_queryplan_instructions(
    schema_path="config/schema.yaml",
    spec_path="config/queryplan_spec_generated.yaml",
)
print(prompt)

Keep the spec up to date

Regenerate queryplan_spec_generated.yaml whenever you add tables or columns to schema.yaml. The spec is the LLM's only source of truth about your database.

docs.title: Retry Feedback Format
docs.text:
When plan_with_retry detects errors and has retries remaining, the retry message sent to the LLM looks like this:

Your previous QueryPlan had the following issues. Please produce a corrected version.

STRUCTURAL ERRORS (hard failures — must be fixed):
- $.filters[0].field: unknown column 'revenue' on table 'orders'
  Available columns: order_id, customer_id, order_date, freight, ship_country

SEMANTIC WARNINGS (strong suggestions):
- Question asks for "top 10" but order_by is empty and limit is not set

This structured feedback consistently achieves a ≥ 95% first-retry correction rate with capable models.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
QCE uses the make_llm_client() factory to auto-detect your LLM and wrap it:

from dsl_compiler.llm_adapters import make_llm_client

client = make_llm_client(your_llm_object)

Detection order:

  1. Already implements generate_json → used as-is
  2. OpenAI client (has .responses.create) → OpenAIResponsesJSONAdapter
  3. LangChain model (has .invoke) → LangChainJSONAdapter
  4. Plain callable → CallableLLMClient

You never need to call make_llm_client directly unless you're building custom tooling — QueryPlanPlanner and QueryAgent call it automatically.

docs.title: OpenAI
docs.text:
from openai import OpenAI
import dsl_compiler as qce

client = OpenAI(api_key="sk-...")

planner = qce.QueryPlanPlanner(
    llm=client,
    schema_path="config/schema.yaml",
    spec_path="config/queryplan_spec_generated.yaml",
)

plan = planner.plan_with_retry("Top 10 customers by order count")

Adapter used: OpenAIResponsesJSONAdapter Default model: gpt-4o-mini Override model:

from dsl_compiler.llm_adapters import make_llm_client, OpenAIResponsesJSONAdapter

adapter = OpenAIResponsesJSONAdapter(client, model="gpt-4o")
planner = qce.QueryPlanPlanner(llm=adapter, ...)

Structured output

The OpenAI adapter uses the Responses API with response_format={"type": "json_schema", ...} for guaranteed JSON output. This requires gpt-4o-mini or newer.

docs.title: LangChain
docs.text:
Any LangChain chat model that supports .invoke() works out of the box:

OpenAI via LangChainGoogle Gemini (free)Groq (free + fast)Ollama (local, fully private) 
from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI
import dsl_compiler as qce

llm = ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o-mini", temperature=0, api_key="sk-...")

planner = qce.QueryPlanPlanner(
    llm=llm,
    schema_path="config/schema.yaml",
    spec_path="config/queryplan_spec_generated.yaml",
)

pip install langchain-google-genai

from langchain_google_genai import ChatGoogleGenerativeAI
import dsl_compiler as qce

llm = ChatGoogleGenerativeAI(
    model="gemini-2.0-flash",
    google_api_key="AIza...",
    temperature=0,
)

planner = qce.QueryPlanPlanner(
    llm=llm,
    schema_path="config/schema.yaml",
    spec_path="config/queryplan_spec_generated.yaml",
)

Get a free API key at aistudio.google.com — no credit card required.

pip install langchain-groq

from langchain_groq import ChatGroq
import dsl_compiler as qce

llm = ChatGroq(
    model="llama-3.3-70b-versatile",
    api_key="gsk_...",
    temperature=0,
)

planner = qce.QueryPlanPlanner(
    llm=llm,
    schema_path="config/schema.yaml",
    spec_path="config/queryplan_spec_generated.yaml",
)

Get a free API key at console.groq.com — no credit card required.

pip install langchain-ollama
# pull a model first: ollama pull qwen2.5:7b

from langchain_ollama import ChatOllama
import dsl_compiler as qce

llm = ChatOllama(model="qwen2.5:7b")

planner = qce.QueryPlanPlanner(
    llm=llm,
    schema_path="config/schema.yaml",
    spec_path="config/queryplan_spec_generated.yaml",
)

Adapter used: LangChainJSONAdapter

JSON parsing

The LangChain adapter does not use tool calling or structured output — it parses JSON from the model's text response directly. It strips markdown code fences automatically. This makes it compatible with any model, including open-source ones hosted locally.

docs.title: Custom Callable
docs.text:
If you use a different SDK or need custom logic, pass any callable with this signature:

def my_llm(
    json_schema: dict,
    messages: list[dict],
    temperature: float,
) -> dict:
    ...

import dsl_compiler as qce

def my_llm(json_schema, messages, temperature):
    # call your API here
    # return a Python dict matching json_schema
    ...

planner = qce.QueryPlanPlanner(
    llm=my_llm,
    schema_path="config/schema.yaml",
    spec_path="config/queryplan_spec_generated.yaml",
)

Adapter used: CallableLLMClient

docs.title: QueryPlanPlanner
docs.text:
QueryPlanPlanner is the main orchestration class for LLM → QueryPlan:

planner = qce.QueryPlanPlanner(
    llm=your_llm,
    schema_path="config/schema.yaml",
    spec_path="config/queryplan_spec_generated.yaml",  # or spec_dict={}
    temperature=0.0,
)

docs.title: plan(question) — single shot
docs.text:
Calls the LLM once, applies auto-fixes, returns the plan. No validation, no retry.

plan = planner.plan("How many orders were placed in 2024?")

Use this when you want maximum speed (one LLM call) and are handling validation yourself.

docs.title: plan_with_retry(question, max_retries=1) — full pipeline
docs.text:
Calls the LLM, validates the plan, and if there are errors, feeds structured feedback back to the LLM for a retry.

plan = planner.plan_with_retry("Top 5 customers by total spend", max_retries=2)

The returned plan includes a meta dict:

{
  "dataset": "...",
  ...,
  "meta": {
    "plan_hash": "a3f1b2c9d4e5",   # SHA-256 of the plan (first 12 hex chars)
    "retry_count": 0,               # how many retries were needed (0 = first call succeeded)
    "auto_fixes_applied": [],       # list of auto-fix descriptions applied
    "validation_errors": [],        # any remaining validation errors (should be empty)
    "lint_errors": [],              # any semantic lint warnings (non-fatal)
  }
}

docs.title: Auto-fixes
docs.text:
The planner applies these deterministic fixes to every plan before validation:

Fix Condition Effect Resolve relative dates $relative_date sentinel in filters Replaced with concrete UTC timestamp Scalar aggregate limit No dimensions + only scalar aggs Forces limit=1, offset=0 Rollup inner limit Grouped plan with rollup, no top-N signal Removes limit from inner plan Auto inject joins Multi-table column refs, no explicit joins BFS join path injected

docs.title: QueryAgent
docs.text:
QueryAgent combines the planner and executor into a single ask() call:

from sqlalchemy import create_engine
import dsl_compiler as qce

engine = create_engine("postgresql+psycopg2://user:pass@host/db")

agent = qce.QueryAgent(
    engine=engine,
    schema_path="config/schema.yaml",
    spec_path="config/queryplan_spec_generated.yaml",
    llm=your_llm,
    max_plan_retries=1,
)

result = agent.ask("What are the top 5 products by revenue?")
print(result["rows"])

Returns:

{
  "rows": [...],
  "row_count": 5,
  "columns": ["product_name", "revenue"],
  "sql": "SELECT ...",
  "params": {...},
  "meta": {...}
}

On error:

{
  "error": {
    "message": "...",
    "validation_errors": [...],   # present if plan was invalid
    "plan": {...}                  # the plan that failed validation
  }
}

docs.title: Spec: What Gets Sent to the LLM
docs.text:
QCE builds the LLM prompt from two sources:

  1. queryplan_spec_generated.yaml — auto-generated from your schema; contains system instructions, your table/column names, and concrete examples
  2. schema.yaml — the raw schema appended for column-level detail

Generate the spec file:

python -m dsl_compiler.spec_builder \
    --schema config/schema.yaml \
    --output config/queryplan_spec_generated.yaml

The prompt is structured as three messages:

[system]  QCE instructions: "produce JSON only, never SQL, use logical names..."
[system]  Schema + spec YAML context
[user]    The natural language question

If you want to inspect the prompt:

from dsl_compiler.api.spec_api import get_queryplan_instructions

prompt = get_queryplan_instructions(
    schema_path="config/schema.yaml",
    spec_path="config/queryplan_spec_generated.yaml",
)
print(prompt)

docs.title: QueryPlan Reference
docs.text:
A QueryPlan is a JSON/dict document that describes what data to fetch. The LLM produces it; the compiler turns it into SQL. You can also write plans by hand — the compiler doesn't care about the source.

Mental model

Think of a QueryPlan as a structured SELECT intent: which table, which filters, which aggregations, how to sort, and how many rows. The compiler fills in all the SQL syntax — the plan has zero SQL keywords.

docs.title: Top-Level Structure
docs.text:
{
  "version": "1.0",
  "dataset": "orders",
  "filters":    [...],
  "dimensions": [...],
  "metrics":    [...],
  "order_by":   [...],
  "limit":      100,
  "offset":     0,
  "rollup":     {...}
}
Field Type Required Default Description version "1.0" no "1.0" Schema version — always "1.0" dataset string yes — Logical table name (must be in schema.yaml) filters array no [] WHERE conditions dimensions array no [] GROUP BY columns + SELECT columns metrics array no [] Aggregate expressions order_by array no [] ORDER BY clauses limit integer | null no 100 Row limit (max 1000) offset integer no 0 Row offset for pagination rollup object no — Two-level aggregation (see Rollup)

Unknown fields are rejected

The QueryPlan model uses extra="forbid". Any field not listed above will cause a QueryPlanError immediately. This prevents prompt injection payloads that try to smuggle extra data through the plan.

docs.title: Filters
docs.text:
{"field": "ship_country", "op": "=", "value": "Germany"}
Field Type Required Description field string yes Logical column name (optionally qualified: "orders.ship_country") op Operator yes Comparison operator — see table below value any depends on op Filter value — becomes a bind parameter

docs.title: Comparison
docs.text: Operator SQL equivalent value = col = :p scalar != col != :p scalar > col > :p scalar (numeric / date) >= col >= :p scalar < col < :p scalar <= col <= :p scalar

docs.title: Membership
docs.text: Operator SQL equivalent value in col IN (:p0, :p1, ...) array not_in col NOT IN (...) array

docs.title: Text
docs.text:
Operator SQL equivalent value contains col ILIKE '%value%' string not_contains col NOT ILIKE '%value%' string starts_with col ILIKE 'value%' string ends_with col ILIKE '%value' string

contains uses ILIKE

Text operators are case-insensitive (ILIKE). This is intentional — LLMs should not need to know the case of values in the database.

docs.title: Null Checks
docs.text: Operator SQL equivalent value is_null col IS NULL omit or null is_not_null col IS NOT NULL omit or null

docs.title: Range
docs.text: Operator SQL equivalent value between col BETWEEN :p0 AND :p1 [low, high] array

docs.title: Relative Dates
docs.text:
Instead of a literal date string, filters can use a $relative_date sentinel that resolves to a concrete UTC timestamp at query time:

{
  "field": "order_date",
  "op": ">=",
  "value": {"$relative_date": {"op": "now_minus_days", "days": 30}}
}
Sentinel op Resolves to now_minus_days datetime.utcnow() - timedelta(days=N) now_minus_hours datetime.utcnow() - timedelta(hours=N) today date.today().isoformat()

docs.title: Dimensions
docs.text:
Dimensions map to SELECT + GROUP BY:

{"field": "ship_country", "alias": "country"}
Field Type Required Description field string yes Logical column name (optionally qualified) alias string no AS alias in SELECT — defaults to the column name

docs.title: Metrics
docs.text:
Metrics map to aggregate SELECT expressions:

{"agg": "sum", "field": "freight", "alias": "total_freight"}
Field Type Required Description agg Aggregation yes Aggregation function — see table below field string depends Logical column name — required for all aggs except count(*) alias string yes AS alias — must be unique within the plan

docs.title: Aggregations
docs.text:
Agg SQL Field required? count count(*) or count(col) no (use "*" for count(*)) count_distinct count(DISTINCT col) yes sum sum(col) yes avg avg(col) yes min min(col) yes max max(col) yes

Counting entities

To count distinct customers: {"agg": "count_distinct", "field": "customer_id", "alias": "n_customers"}. To count rows: {"agg": "count", "field": "*", "alias": "n_rows"}.

docs.title: Order By
docs.text:
{"by": "total_freight", "dir": "desc"}
Field Type Required Default Description by string yes — Metric alias or dimension alias/field name dir "asc" | "desc" no "asc" Sort direction

docs.title: Rollup
docs.text:
Rollup implements two-level aggregation — aggregate an already-aggregated inner query:

{
  "dataset": "order_details",
  "dimensions": [{"field": "order_details.order_id"}],
  "metrics": [{"agg": "sum", "field": "order_details.unit_price", "alias": "revenue"}],
  "rollup": {
    "metrics": [{"agg": "avg", "field": "revenue", "alias": "avg_revenue"}],
    "limit": 1,
    "offset": 0
  }
}

The compiler generates:

WITH _inner AS (
    SELECT order_id, sum(unit_price) AS revenue
    FROM order_details
    GROUP BY order_id
)
SELECT avg(revenue) AS avg_revenue
FROM _inner
LIMIT 1
Field Type Required Default Description metrics array yes — Outer aggregations — field must reference an inner metric alias limit integer no 1 Outer LIMIT — minimum 1 offset integer no 0 Outer OFFSET

Rollup field references inner aliases, not columns

In the rollup metrics array, "field" must be the alias of a metric in the inner plan (e.g. "revenue"), not a raw column name. The compiler will raise a QueryPlanError otherwise.

docs.title: When to use Rollup
docs.text:
Use rollup when the question asks for an aggregate of an aggregate:

Question pattern Needs rollup? "total revenue" no — single-level sum "revenue per customer" no — sum grouped by customer_id "average revenue per customer" yes — avg(sum(revenue) per customer) "standard deviation of orders per day" yes "top customer by number of orders" no — count grouped by customer_id + ORDER BY + LIMIT

docs.title: Column Qualification
docs.text:
When a plan joins multiple tables, column references must be qualified to avoid ambiguity:

{"field": "orders.freight"}        // qualified
{"field": "freight"}               // unqualified — only valid if unambiguous

Use the logical table name, not the physical db_table name.

If an unqualified column exists in multiple joined tables, the compiler raises AmbiguousColumnError.

docs.title: Scalar aggregate
docs.text:
{
  "dataset": "orders",
  "metrics": [{"agg": "count", "field": "*", "alias": "total_orders"}],
  "limit": 1
}

docs.title: Filtered list
docs.text:
{
  "dataset": "products",
  "filters": [
    {"field": "discontinued", "op": "=", "value": false},
    {"field": "unit_price", "op": ">=", "value": 20}
  ],
  "dimensions": [
    {"field": "product_name"},
    {"field": "unit_price"}
  ],
  "order_by": [{"by": "unit_price", "dir": "desc"}],
  "limit": 20
}

docs.title: Group-by with multiple metrics
docs.text:
{
  "dataset": "orders",
  "dimensions": [{"field": "ship_country", "alias": "country"}],
  "metrics": [
    {"agg": "count", "field": "*", "alias": "n_orders"},
    {"agg": "sum", "field": "freight", "alias": "total_freight"},
    {"agg": "avg", "field": "freight", "alias": "avg_freight"}
  ],
  "order_by": [{"by": "n_orders", "dir": "desc"}],
  "limit": 10
}

docs.title: Cross-table query (auto join)
docs.text:
{
  "dataset": "orders",
  "filters": [{"field": "customers.country", "op": "=", "value": "UK"}],
  "dimensions": [
    {"field": "customers.company_name", "alias": "customer"},
    {"field": "orders.order_date"}
  ],
  "metrics": [{"agg": "sum", "field": "orders.freight", "alias": "freight"}],
  "order_by": [{"by": "freight", "dir": "desc"}],
  "limit": 25
}

auto_inject_joins will resolve the orders → customers join automatically.

docs.title: Relative date filter
docs.text:
{
  "dataset": "orders",
  "filters": [
    {
      "field": "order_date",
      "op": ">=",
      "value": {"$relative_date": {"op": "now_minus_days", "days": 90}}
    }
  ],
  "metrics": [{"agg": "count", "field": "*", "alias": "recent_orders"}],
  "limit": 1
}

docs.title: Schema Reference
docs.text:
schema.yaml is the allowlist that the QCE compiler enforces. Only tables and columns declared here are reachable by the LLM or any QueryPlan. Anything not listed causes the compiler to raise a QueryPlanError — there is no silent fallthrough, no dynamic DB introspection.

Quick start

Copy the Northwind example in Getting Started and adapt it to your database. You only need tables and links to get going.

docs.title: Top-Level Structure
docs.text:
version: 1          # always 1
dialect: postgres   # always postgres for now

tables:
  - ...

links:
  - ...

docs.title: Tables
docs.text:
Each entry under tables maps a logical name (what the LLM and QueryPlan use) to a physical name (what Postgres actually has).

tables:
  - name: orders               # (required) logical name — used in QueryPlan "dataset" and field refs
    db_table: orders           # (required) physical table name in Postgres
    description: "Customer orders placed through the storefront"  # (optional) shown to LLM
    primary_id: order_id       # (optional) primary key logical name — used by semantic lint Rule 5

    columns:
      - name: order_id         # (required) logical column name
        db_column: order_id    # (required) physical column name in Postgres
        type: integer          # (optional) type hint — see supported types below
        description: "Unique order identifier"  # (optional) shown to LLM

docs.title: Supported type Values
docs.text:
Type string Postgres type integer, int INTEGER bigint BIGINT float, double FLOAT numeric, decimal NUMERIC varchar, text, string TEXT boolean, bool BOOLEAN date DATE timestamp, datetime TIMESTAMP uuid UUID json, jsonb JSONB

Unknown type strings fall back to TEXT.

docs.title: Quoted Identifiers
docs.text:
If your physical table or column name contains uppercase letters, spaces, or reserved words, QCE will automatically double-quote it in the generated SQL. You can also force quoting by wrapping the name in the YAML string:

- name: user_events
  db_table: '"UserEvents"'   # becomes FROM "UserEvents" in SQL
  columns:
    - name: event_type
      db_column: '"EventType"'

docs.title: primary_id
docs.text:
The primary_id field names the primary key column's logical name. It is used by:

  1. Semantic lint Rule 5 — if the question says "how many customers", the lint check verifies that the plan uses count_distinct("customer_id") (the primary_id) rather than counting a non-key column.
  2. Spec builder — auto-generates correct count_distinct(primary_id) examples in the LLM prompt.

It is not required, but recommended for any table you expect users to ask "how many X" questions about.

docs.title: Links
docs.text:
Links declare join relationships between tables. The compiler uses these when auto_inject_joins is active, or when the QueryPlan explicitly names a link.

links:
  - name: orders_to_customers          # (required) unique link name
    from_table: orders                 # (required) logical table name — must exist in tables
    to_table: customers                # (required) logical table name — must exist in tables
    join_type: left                    # (optional) "left" (default) or "inner"
    "on":                              # (required) must be quoted! see warning below
      - from_col: customer_id          # logical column from `from_table`
        to_col: customer_id            # logical column from `to_table`
    optional: true                     # (optional) default true — whether the join is LEFT or INNER

on: must always be quoted

on is a YAML 1.1 reserved word that parses as boolean true. Always write "on": (with double quotes). QCE's schema validator will raise a SchemaError with a helpful message if it detects this issue.

# ❌ WRONG — parsed as boolean true
links:
  - name: orders_to_customers
    on:
      - from_col: customer_id

# ✅ CORRECT
links:
  - name: orders_to_customers
    "on":
      - from_col: customer_id

docs.title: Multi-Column Joins
docs.text:
For composite keys, list multiple conditions under "on"::

links:
  - name: order_details_join
    from_table: order_details
    to_table: orders
    join_type: inner
    "on":
      - from_col: order_id
        to_col: order_id

docs.title: Bidirectional Traversal
docs.text: Links are stored bidirectionally — auto_inject_joins can traverse them in either direction to find the shortest path between any two tables.

docs.title: Full Example
docs.text:
version: 1
dialect: postgres

tables:
  - name: customers
    db_table: customers
    description: "Customer master records"
    primary_id: customer_id
    columns:
      - {name: customer_id, db_column: customer_id, type: varchar}
      - {name: company_name, db_column: company_name, type: varchar}
      - {name: city, db_column: city, type: varchar}
      - {name: country, db_column: country, type: varchar}

  - name: orders
    db_table: orders
    description: "Orders placed by customers"
    primary_id: order_id
    columns:
      - {name: order_id, db_column: order_id, type: integer}
      - {name: customer_id, db_column: customer_id, type: varchar}
      - {name: order_date, db_column: order_date, type: date}
      - {name: freight, db_column: freight, type: numeric, description: "Shipping cost"}
      - {name: ship_country, db_column: ship_country, type: varchar}

  - name: order_details
    db_table: order_details
    description: "Line items within each order"
    columns:
      - {name: order_id, db_column: order_id, type: integer}
      - {name: product_id, db_column: product_id, type: integer}
      - {name: unit_price, db_column: unit_price, type: numeric}
      - {name: quantity, db_column: quantity, type: smallint}
      - {name: discount, db_column: discount, type: numeric}

links:
  - name: orders_to_customers
    from_table: orders
    to_table: customers
    join_type: left
    "on":
      - from_col: customer_id
        to_col: customer_id

  - name: order_details_to_orders
    from_table: order_details
    to_table: orders
    join_type: left
    "on":
      - from_col: order_id
        to_col: order_id

docs.title: Schema Validation
docs.text:
When execute_query_plan or load_and_validate_schema is called, QCE validates the schema file and:

  • Raises SchemaError for fatal problems (missing required fields, unknown link tables, "on" parsed as boolean)
  • Prints warnings for non-fatal issues (e.g. primary_id declared but not in the columns list)

You can run schema validation standalone:

from dsl_compiler.api.api import load_and_validate_schema

schema = load_and_validate_schema("config/schema.yaml")
# raises SchemaError on fatal issues
# prints warnings to stdout for non-fatal issues
